data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Microsoft, OpenAI Chart New Path
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Microsoft, OpenAI (MSFT) redefine ties in shift to for-profit model. (00:24) Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) jumps on report Paramount Skydance (PSKY) preparing offer. (01:37) Apple Watch (AAPL) hypertension detection to roll out next week after FDA nod- report. (02:56)

Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and OpenAI have signed a non-binding memorandum of understanding to redefine their relationship, paving the way for OpenAI to restructure into a for-profit company.

According to an official statement, “OpenAI’s planned evolution will see the existing OpenAI nonprofit both control a Public Benefit Corporation (PBC) and share directly in its success. OpenAI started as a nonprofit, remains one today, and will continue to be one—with the nonprofit holding the authority that guides our future.”

OpenAI’s nonprofit arm stands to receive over $100 billion, or about 20% of the $500 billion valuation the company is targeting in private markets.

Microsoft and OpenAI are working toward a final agreement.

Microsoft (MSFT) has been a key OpenAI backer, investing $1 billion in 2019 and $10 billion in 2023. The earlier deal gave Microsoft exclusive rights to sell OpenAI tools through Azure and have preferred access to its technology.

This year, however, OpenAI began diversifying by building its own data center project, “Stargate,” and signing major cloud deals with Oracle (ORCL) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) worth more than $300 billion.

The restructuring still requires approval from regulators in California and Delaware.

Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) jumped 29% on Thursday after a report that Paramount Skydance (NASDAQ:PSKY) is preparing a majority cash bid. Paramount Skydance climbed 15.5%.

According to a WSJ report on Thursday, which cited people familiar with the matter, the offer will be for the entire company, including its cable networks and movie studio. The bid will be backed by the Ellison family.

According to CNBC's David Faber, a Paramount Skydance bid for Warner Bros. Discovery may come as soon as next week.

The potential bid comes after Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) in June confirmed that it would separate into two companies, with one focused on streaming and the other on its television networks.

A deal would combine Paramount's CBS News with Warner Bros. CNN as well as Warner Bros. studio with Paramount studio.

Paramount Skydance may be trying to make a bid before Warner Bros.' planned split in an attempt to pre-empt a possible bidding war for the studio and streaming unit that could include Amazon.com (AMZN) and Apple (AAPL), according to the WSJ report.

A Paramount Skydance (NASDAQ:PSKY) bid would also follow after Oracle Corp. co-founder Larry Ellison's wealth jumped by more than $100 billion on Wednesday after the stock 36% surge, at one point making him the richest person in the world, over taking Elon Musk, according to the Bloomberg's billionaire index.

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) gets the go ahead from U.S. Food and Drug Administration for its new hypertension detection tool for Apple Watch. It'll roll out next week.

The tool uses data from the Apple Watch’s optical heart sensor to analyze how a user’s blood vessels respond to heartbeats over a 30-day time period, Apple reported.

The hypertension detection tool has been developed using machine learning trained on data from studies with over 100,000 participants and is compatible with Apple Watch Series 9, 10, 11, Ultra 2, and Ultra 3.

The tool will notify the smartwatch user if any signs of chronic hypertension are detected and would encourage sharing results with a medical provider.

The feature will be available in 150 regions, including the US, EU, and Hong Kong.

Apple unveiled its new Apple Watch Series 11, Ultra 3, and SE models at its latest product launch event earlier this week.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

United CEO sees Spirit Airlines going out of business - report

Tesla engineer quits after 8 years, criticizes Elon Musk's leadership in exit letter

Nvidia's supplier SK hynix ready with next-gen memory chip for AI

Catalyst watch:

  • Take-Two Interactive (TTWO) will release the Borderlands 4 videogame for PlayStation 5, Windows, and Xbox Series X/S, with a Nintendo Switch 2 release following on October 3.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is up 0.2% at $62/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.3% at $115,000. Gold is up 0.2% at $3,638.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.2% and the DAX is down 0.3%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: RH (NYSE:RH) -7% - Shares sank after the upscale furniture retailer missed Q2 expectations and cut its full-year forecast, citing new U.S. tariffs, delayed catalog releases, and a weak housing market.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 10:00 am Consumer Sentiment

And finally, our most recent news quiz just dropped. If you haven’t checked it out, it’s 10 questions all about the news of the week. One of the questions this week is about a story we talked about today on Wall Street Breakfast but it has a twist. It says: Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) soared on a report that Paramount Skydance (PSKY) is preparing an all-cash bid for the company. If the deal goes through, which planet will the combined company NOT have the rights to?

I can’t wait to see if you get it right. I’ll leave a link to the quiz in the show notes section.

data.title: BP: Strategic Realignment And Cost-Saving Initiatives May Expand Profitability And Cash Flow Margins
data.description:
Introduction

Based on my analysis, BP p.l.c. (NYSE:BP) presents an attractive investment opportunity for investors who are looking to gain exposure to the energy sector. Apart from the BP's strategic realignment, the company is also committed to improving its profitability by further optimizing its operations and cost structure. Additionally, if BP can maintain its current performance, there is no doubt that shareholders will continue to see attractive returns in the form of repurchases and dividends. Based on my valuation analysis, BP presents a potential upside of more than 40%. Hence, in this report, I will demonstrate why investors should consider BP in their portfolio.

Company Overview

BP is one of the world's most renowned energy companies, based in the United Kingdom. Incorporated in 1908, BP now has more than 100.5k full-time employees around the world, and the company's headquarters is currently in London, United Kingdom.

Based on the company's filings, BP's operations are segregated into the following segments: (1) Gas & Low Carbon Energy, (2) Oil Production & Operations, (3) Customers & Products, (4) Other Businesses & Corporate Activities. Most of BP's sales are derived from Gas & Low Carbon Energy and Customers & Product Segments; these account for 18.9% and 80.4%, respectively. It is important to note that these are revenues after eliminating sales between segments. Separately, close to 66% of BP's revenues are derived outside the United States.

Latest Developments

In the most recent quarter, BP generated $46.62 billion, representing a decline of -1.4% y/y. In 2Q25, BP also saw deteriorated topline performance in most of its core segments. With the exception of BP's Gas & Low Carbon Energy segment, BP's individual segments saw declines of between -8.7% to -65.3%.

During this period, profitability improved primarily due to lower expenses. As a percentage of total revenues, total purchases fell to 57.64% from 61.08% in the same period last year, whereas production and manufacturing expenses fell to 13.2% as compared to 14.15% in the same period last year. Net income grew more than 26.5x as compared to the same period last year, while net income margin expanded by 400 bps to 4.14% as compared to 0.15% in the same period last year.

Persisting Demand For Black Gold Supports BP's Strategic Realignment On Traditional Sources Of Energy

Even though the world has invested heavily in alternative energy in recent years, multiple indicators continue to suggest that demand for traditional oil and gas remains surprisingly high. In a recent study conducted by OIES, oil demand is unlikely to peak anytime soon, and global oil demand is expected to expand by 6.2 million barrels per day to hit 108 mb/d by FY2030 from 101.8 mb/d in FY2023.

The primary drivers identified by OIES for the continued expansion are: (1) increasing non-OECD demand and (2) persisting growth in petrochemicals, jet fuel, and kerosene. Current studies on gas also revealed a similar trend. According to IGU, demand for gas will continue to trek towards the upside primarily due to support from non-OECD countries.

More importantly, BP recognizes that traditional sources of energy are here to stay and has already started to strategically reposition itself; for now, BP aims to generate higher returns from oil and gas instead of investing aggressively in renewables, which generally have lesser returns in the short term. This is also because the company realized that it has underperformed its peers that are less aggressive in renewables.

As such, in the foreseeable future, BP will reinvest primarily in traditional oil and gas; investment into renewables is estimated to be less than 5% of the company's total capital expenditures. Thus far, for FY2025, BP already has 10 exploration discoveries in multiple countries such as Brazil, India, and Egypt and another 5 new oil and gas projects in Angola, United States, Trinidad, and Tobago. Pertaining to BP's renewables portfolio, the company will be divesting many of its less efficient operations, such as its US onshore wind and Australian Renewable Energy Hub.

Profitability and Cash Flow Margins May Further Expand Due to Cost Reduction Initiatives

Apart from the company's strategic realignment, the company continues to make progress in its cost reduction program. As of 2Q25, BP has already delivered $1.7 billion out of its $4 billion OPEX reduction target; more than 75% of the achieved reductions are due to supply chain optimization and organizational transformation.

By the end of 2025, BP will further streamline its operations, eliminating another 6.2k full-time employees. Additionally, BP will continue to invest in artificial intelligence and automation to further reduce its overall costs; recently, BP utilized generative AI for its Azerbaijan operations, reducing the total time for well planning by 90%. Separately, BP will work with Palantir to further optimize its refinery operations.

As a reference, the $4 billion reduction represents approximately 2.14% of the company's annualized revenue. More importantly, if the company is able to maintain its current traction, BP's profit and cash flow margins can potentially further expand. In fact, BP's profitability is already improving; the company's most recent net income margin expanded by more than 400 bps as compared to the same period last year. From an FCF perspective, BP believes that the company's FCF will be able to grow at a CAGR of 20% per annum till FY2027.

BP Continues To Return Value to Shareholders Despite Ongoing Realignment

It is important to note that BP has been consistently returning value to its shareholders in recent years. For the past ten years, BP has returned $87.15 billion to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends. As a reference, $87.15 billion is more than 95% of the company's current market capitalization.

Looking ahead, shareholders should expect to see BP continue returning its excess cash through both share repurchases and dividends. In fact, BP recently announced a $750 million share buyback and a 4% increase in dividends. Therefore, as long as BP maintains its current traction and progress, it is reasonable for investors to expect BP to continue returning value through share repurchases and dividends.

Valuation Analysis Implies An Upside Potential Of More Than 40%

From a relative valuation perspective, BP can be considered slightly more attractive as compared to its peers. Currently, BP is trading at a P/OCF and EV/EBITDA of 3.75x and 5.4x as compared to the industry median of 5.26x and 6.84x. That being said, it is important to note that the company's forward P/E is currently trading at 15.09x which is above the industry median of 13.24x.

To ascertain the valuation of BP, I have used the following assumptions and variables:

  • Forward P/E to remain at 15.09x. This multiple is based on BP's current forward P/E. Given that BP has multiple upcoming positive catalysts due to its cost reduction initiatives and strategic repositioning, I believe that BP will not see any P/E multiple contraction.
  • FY2027 EPS of $3.26 per share. This is based on current consensus estimates. FY2027 EPS is chosen to reflect the potential success of the company's cost reduction initiatives and strategic repositioning. Additionally, it is likely that BP's profit level will start to normalize by FY2027.

Based on these assumptions and variables, BP's share price should be worth $49.19 per share by the end of FY2027. Currently, BP trades at $34.76, implying a potential upside of 41.51%.

Closing Remarks & Investment Risks

Overall, BP presents an attractive opportunity for investors who are looking to gain exposure in the energy sector. If BP's strategic realignment succeeds, there is a strong possibility that the company will be able to improve its profitability and cash flow metrics.

Moreover, BP is not only just focusing on the top line. The company's cost-saving initiatives through optimization and experimentation of new technologies like artificial intelligence will further support its quest for higher returns. More importantly, BP is currently cheap and offers a potential upside of more than 40%.

That being said, we are currently in a period of great macroeconomic uncertainty; there are many indicators that suggest that the deterioration of the economy is far from over. If macroeconomic conditions worsen, there is a strong likelihood that oil prices may plunge. Unfortunately, BP's ability to improve its cash flow and profitability metrics is highly dependent on oil prices. Therefore, investors who are interested in BP should closely monitor the global macroeconomy and oil prices. If oil prices further capitulate, it will render my thesis invalid.

data.title: BLOX: Cash In On This New Crypto ETF And Collect A High Yield Every Week
data.description:
Introduction

For years, I remained skeptical about crypto assets, but recently, I've grown to realize that the digital asset is likely here to stay. Companies are adopting this, and I can honestly say I regret not investing heavily in Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD) after playing around with it years ago.

But with the recent emergence of covered call ETFs with exposure to crypto, and me being a dividend-focused investor, this makes it easier to get portfolio diversification while being able to collect income.

In this article, I introduce a new covered call ETF that gives investors just that: the Nicholas Crypto Income ETF (NYSEARCA:BLOX).

While the fund is high risk with a very short track record and the potential to see NAV erosion over time, like a lot of other covered call funds, it may be suitable for those less risk-averse.

In this article, I discuss BLOX's investment strategy and why this new covered call ETF that pays more than a 6% yield on a weekly basis may be attractive depending on your investment strategy.

Who is BLOX?

Nicholas Crypto Income ETF is a new, weekly-paying ETF that was recently launched roughly three months ago with an inception date of June 16, 2025. The fund is actively managed, and its main purpose is to give investors the potential to see capital appreciation.

I found this particularly attractive because most covered call funds' main objective is to provide income and capital appreciation second. So far, BLOX has done that, up an impressive 18.78% at the time of writing.

But as previously mentioned, their track record is very short, so this should be taken with a grain of salt if considering. Moreover, the fund does not directly invest in Bitcoin or any other crypto assets but instead invests in companies with direct exposure to crypto. They also invest in those that conduct crypto mining, blockchain technology, or crypto trading platforms.

BLOX currently has 72 holdings that include notable companies like Robinhood Markets (HOOD), Coinbase Global (COIN), NVIDIA (NVDA), Riot Platforms (RIOT), and Galaxy Digital (GLXY), all of which make up their top 10 holdings. VanEck Bitcoin ETF (HODL) makes up the largest portion of BLOX's portfolio at 18.15%.

Comparison

There are other weekly-paying ETFs with crypto exposure. Two are the Roundhill COIN WeeklyPay ETF (COIW) & REX COIN Growth & Income ETF (COII). Both have a slightly cheaper cost setup with expense ratios of 0.99% compared to 1.03% for BLOX.

I usually look for funds with an expense ratio less than 1%, but for some, this may not be as important due to the income these funds usually generate.

Contrarily, COIW & COII's portfolios mostly consist of U.S. Treasury bills, while BLOX holds actual companies.

And this makes them less sensitive to interest rates and more likely to capture upside if their underlying assets see strong capital appreciation over the long term.

Here's how some of their top holdings have performed vs. the S&P (SP500) over the past year. HOOD has significantly outperformed, up over 400%, while HODL has also performed well, up over 100%. Going forward, BLOX is likely to see solid price appreciation if its holdings continue to appreciate.

Distributions & Taxes

As stated previously, BLOX pays distributions weekly, making them particularly attractive for those in search of income. Although their track record is short, the fund has paid between $0.13 and $0.16 a week since inception.

Another metric passive income investors may find attractive, especially younger investors, is that most of the fund's distributions are considered return of capital, meaning taxes are deferred until you decide to sell.

This makes them suitable for a taxable account, meaning you can receive distributions now if you're looking to live off them instead of waiting until 59 1/2 to withdraw tax-free.

Below is their latest distribution at the time of writing, paid on September 2nd, which was 100% ROC.

However, the distribution the week prior, while mostly ROC, also consisted of net investment income, which is taxed differently.

Net investment income is taxed depending on your modified adjusted gross income, or MAGI. But you would only be taxed if your NII & MAGI are higher than the set amount set forth by the IRS.

  • Single - $200,000
  • Married filing jointly - $150,000
  • Married filing separately - $125,000

Here are two different examples:

So, depending on your tax situation, this may be a suitable investment if you're younger or a retiree.

Risks

The biggest risk when investing in covered call funds is NAV erosion, which leads to price decay. Moreover, since BLOX is roughly 3 months old, they have limited data to go on, making them less appealing for risk-averse investors.

And thirdly, crypto assets are typically highly volatile, and BLOX could see underperformance as a result. Their expense ratio above 1% may also make them less attractive, as there are other cheaper funds.

Conclusion

With the growing popularity of covered call ETFs, my goal is to introduce readers to these, giving them additional exposure to income-generating assets.

Moreover, because BLOX is new, investors may be catching them at a good time, as the fund could see solid upside if its underlying holdings continue to outperform.

And while they may be considered less appealing for investors who are risk-averse, their weekly distributions and capital appreciation first investment approach may be suitable for those seeking growth & income.

data.title: Grupo Financiero Galicia: Prepare For Volatility As Argentina Faces Political Turmoil
data.description:
After a first quarter in which Galicia's (NASDAQ:GGAL) profits fell 63% Y/Y, which previously led me to cut my rating on GGAL, this last quarter was no exception. A further contraction in revenues and profits this Q2, despite an increase in deposits and loans, leads us to conclude that this will definitely not be the year for Argentine banks. As for the last quarter reported, it also showed a contraction in margins and profitability levels across the board in local banks, showing they are finally feeling the pressure to adapt to work as banks again, financing the private sector.

Meanwhile, Argentina's largest private bank posted (as expected) a very weak second quarter result amidst macroeconomic volatility and political uncertainty ahead of the October legislative elections. As the government prepares to gain ground in Congress to continue pushing its pro-market agenda, some specific events recently offset some of the gains made during the first quarter, hampering a potential credit expansion this year.

Volatile macroeconomic context

Positive catalysts from the first half of the year, such as the approval of an historic IMF credit facility and the lifting of foreign exchange restrictions, did not end up bearing fruit (or not til now), beyond an improvement in lending and deposit activity during H1 2025. Seasonal phenomena in the local foreign exchange market, such as the holiday season and the end of dollar inflows from the agricultural harvest, combined with the unwinding of banks' short-term placement mechanisms (LEFIs), caused the dollar's value to skyrocket between June and August.

Unable to further place their cash surpluses in LEFIs, banks rushed to acquire other types of instruments, predominantly in dollars. This further pressured the exchange rate and forced the authorities to intervene to reabsorb part of this monetary surplus in Pesos. However, to incentivize banks, they had to once again offer other short-term instruments (LECAPs and BONCAPs) but at a much higher interest rates than usual, inflicting pain to the productive sector.

As is this weren't enough, the proximity of the provincial elections in Buenos Aires (the country's largest electoral district) added another factor of volatility. Considered a barometer for the congressional elections in October, many investors had heavily discounted a possible unsustainability of the new FX regime and a growing possibility to return to the previous restrictions, leading to more pressure on the FX rate. This was ultimately validated by a terrible election result this Sunday for Milei's government, paving the way for total political uncertainty between now and October.

Q2 2025 results

Diving into GGAL's Q2 quarterly results, we find that the group's consolidated profits suffered a further setback across all segments, with the exception of asset management segment, Fima Funds. Net income fell to AR$173 billion (-70% Y/Y), largely explained by a deterioration in the loan portfolio, which resulted in a higher charge on credit loss provisions (+192% Y/Y). GGAL's net interest income fell 32% Y/Y, due to a drop in government securities income at Banco Galicia (-53% Y/Y), a trend that we have seen since 2024 as the company gradually withdrew from public sector financing. However, as we will see later, this same negative dynamic was also present on other major local banks, which also experienced margin compression and a sharp decline on their profitability metrics.

Looking at the traditional banking business, despite a drop in interest income last year over the last year, we see relative improve Q/Q on ROE of 7.6% vs. 2.4% on Q1, although still far from the 37% recorded in Q2 202 which, is also worthy to mention, was a tremendously atypical year. Deposits continued to grow during the quarter due to fiscal incentives promoted by the government for savings kept "under the mattress" by the public or outside the financial system for many years and the elimination of foreign exchange restrictions in the country. On the other side of the balance, there was a rebound in the private sector loan portfolio, which increased by 123% Y/Y, after having doubled in annual terms during Q1 2025. Meanwhile, capitalization levels for the banking segment remain at very healthy levels, with excess capital over minimum requirements increasing by 68% Y/Y, while the CET1 ratio fell 460 bps on a yearly basis, to 23%, although showing improvement vs. Q1 due to a higher capitalization following Galicia Mas (ex-HSBC) incorporation into equity.

With Naranja X Galicia's has a very attractive retail credit segment. Argentina's largest credit card issuer and GGAL's fintech arm had a meteoric growth over the last year, surpassing 10M clients. However, the company's main growth engine also saw a 30% Y/Y drop in its operating income with ROE showing a jaw dropping correction from the 61% in Q2 2024 to almost 14% last quarter, which also suffered from a higher allowance for loan losses (almost tripled since 2024) and lower net interest income.

For Q3 2025, the company expect the NPL to stabilize around 5% while margins to stay close to those of Q2 2025. The company also adjusted to the downside its loan growth guidance from 50% to 40%. However, as the country's new exchange rate regime is consolidated and macro volatility persist, we may not see a sustained decline in country risk premium. As this milestone is still far from reach, I believe we won't be seeing a significant credit expansion anytime soon nor a significant improvement on margins as many international investors are still on the sidelines, looking for a clearer picture from October.

As already mentioned, macro volatility in Argentina affected most banks in the industry during the quarter, signaling possible market structural challenges and dependecy from external factors rather than companies fundamentals. With the exception of Banco Macro (BMA), which saw an improvement on its profitability and net interest margin performance compared to a year ago, the rest of major banks experienced a contraction in their key metrics.

Valuation: Don't catch a falling knife

The argentine financial sector has been declining so far this year amid this transition period. A widespread collapse following the government's adverse results in the province of Buenos Aires left banks among the ADRs that suffered the most this Monday, with Galicia falling as much as 20% during trading on the NYSE. GGAL's dollar-denominated shares are now trading 60% below their peak level at the beginning of the year, having erased much of the gains made in 2024.

It's almost obvious that at these levels, valuations look much more attractive than they did a few months ago. However, volatility amid the current political context could distort these values ​​between now and the end of the year, which shows us that stocks may not have found their bottom yet, despite current prices and fundamentals. I believe this is even more evident in banks, which are much more affected by the country's macroeconomic environment than the rest, given its exposure to interest rates and foreign exchange market. That said, I believe the downside potential has narrowed significantly in recent months and is currently quite limited, while stocks present a much more attractive upside potential once the correction is over. For this reason, I consider ratifying my Hold rating, pending a clearer political landscape starting in October and a better assessment of the extent to which Milei's government will be able to move forward with its reform agenda next year.

Risks

Electoral uncertainty and the fate of government-led policies represent the greatest short-term threat to banks like Galicia. The October elections will determine the continuation of the exchange rate liberalization and financial sector deregulation policies. Although all indications are that the ruling party will manage to increase its number of seats in Congress and strengthen its position, this will likely be insufficient to achieve consensus and defend the government's agenda.

Furthermore, following the dissolution of the LEFI mechanism, the banking sector is facing a liquidity crisis due to interventions by the Central Bank. Interbank repo rates reached 80% annualized, while many companies face financing costs exceeding 100% per year, threatening the positive growth scenario forecast for the country this year. This poses a challenging environment for GGAL, among other banks, which highly depends on an economic recovery in Argentina. Meanwhile, the accelerated deterioration in asset quality, with Banco Galicia's NPL rising to 4.4% in Q2 2025 (from 2% the previous year), along with a provision coverage ratio fall from 160.3% to 117.9%, are points worth monitoring closely in coming quarters.

Conclusion

Argentina is currently a market that is best to stay away from for now. In particular, its financial sector is facing enormous challenges this year amid extreme macroeconomic volatility and political uncertainty. Even if the pro-market and deregulatory reforms of the Milei administration are sustained, the transition of the banking business will take several months before it is reflected on their bottom line. However, as the political turmoil recedes and we get a clearer macro picture, I see a path toward healthy credit expansion that translate into better margins and higher income. Argentina needs a shock of infrastructure investment and banking sector it may be highly benefited from this in coming years.

data.title: Genmab: A Matter Of Time
data.description:
Genmab (GMAB) reported strong second quarter results last month and raised the full-year revenue and operating income guidance ranges. The increase was primarily driven by the strong performance of royalty products but also by the increasing contribution of net product sales of Epkinly and Tivdak.

The market being hyper-focused on Darzalex has, ironically, been the source of great pain for Genmab shareholders over the last few years. And I am saying ironically because Darzalex has significantly exceeded growth expectations during this period and generated higher-than-expected royalties for Genmab. For example, back in 2022, I estimated that total annual royalties on all products would peak at around $2.5 billion, and Darzalex alone could generate $2.5 billion in royalties for Genmab this year. And it has not peaked yet either.

The problems with Darzalex were well described in the past, and I will not go over them again (reduced royalties on the subcutaneous Halozyme version, loss of arbitration to J&J, and J&J opting out on next-generation GEN3014).

It is best to focus on the future and what I see as an improving growth outlook for Genmab’s maturing and expanding product portfolio and pipeline.

Long-term outlook remains very positive, and Genmab can absorb the loss of Darzalex royalties

The market has largely neglected Epkinly’s commercial success in late-line follicular lymphoma ('FL') and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma ('DLBCL') indications and the recent clinical success in second line+ FL, where it delivered remarkable overall response rate (‘ORR’) and progression-free survival (‘PFS’) data versus the control arm. The results were good enough for the FDA to grant Genmab and its partner AbbVie (ABBV) Priority Review for this application with a PDUFA date in November.

In the second half of the decade, we are looking at a potential steady stream of potential new product launches and new indications for Epkinly, Rina-S, and potentially acasunlimab, which should lead to a significantly reduced reliance on Darzalex royalties. I expect Genmab will be able to absorb the loss of these royalties with the success of Epkinly and Rina-S and with further upside from acasunlimab and other early-stage and/or to be acquired or in-licensed products.

We also have to trust that the cash Genmab is generating is being spent wisely and in a productive manner. So far, so good, as all I have seen the company do in the last few years looks very good to me:

  • The company has been making heavy investments to expand its infrastructure to support in-house drug development and commercialization. It went from collecting royalties on Darzalex and other out-licensed products to a 50:50 profit/loss split with AbbVie on Epkinly and with Pfizer on Tivdak in the U.S. and Japan (while still receiving royalties from other territories), to regaining rights on acasunlimab and committing to paying royalties to partner BioNTech, and finally, to owning full global rights to Rina-S.
  • Management has not been shy to make tough decisions and to discontinue the development of assets that may or may not have been successful in the clinic but that they assessed were not good enough to spend money on. The last one was the OX40 antibody GEN1055. On Q2 the earnings call, they said that the data looked as expected, but that their assessment of its competitiveness was such that it did not warrant further spending on the program. Similarly, a decision was made not to develop GEN3014 (the next-generation Darzalex) after J&J gave up on it, and that was the only reasonable decision to make considering the data this candidate generated.
  • The company has been focused on business development to find assets that can bring revenues as soon as possible and, preferably, well ahead of the loss of Darzalex royalties. The Profound Bio acquisition could do just that. Rina-S was initially seen as a $1 billion+ a year product, and the positive data in the meantime in new indications have increased that estimate to $2 billion+. The acquisition also brought the antibody drug conjugate ('ADC') platform that Genmab was lacking.
  • Genmab has been opportunistically buying back shares. I am not a big fan of buybacks, but sometimes, they make a lot of sense, and this is the case where I believe it does make a lot of sense to buy back shares, as the stock looks significantly undervalued at current levels, and doing so does not threaten any other part of the story.
  • The pipeline is maturing with Epkinly generating strong data in ongoing early-stage and late-stage trials like the mentioned second line+ FL trial. Rina-S is progressing to registrational trials, and Genmab expects to launch Rina-S for the treatment of platinum-resistant ovarian cancer as soon as 2027.

Epkinly is still being viewed as a $3 billion+ a year product at its peak, but the commercial success to date in late-line indications and the positive data in earlier lines point to this number being potentially higher. Epkinly generated $121 million in net sales in the second quarter, a near-$500 million annualized net sales run rate, while still being in the early launch stages for later lines of FL and DLBCL, especially in ex-U.S. markets where partner AbbVie is just getting started.

The great thing about going from late lines of treatment to earlier lines is that each line brings much higher lifetime revenue per patient because earlier line patients have much better outcomes, live longer, and stay on the drug longer.

These effects will accumulate over time, starting in 2026 with second-line+ follicular lymphoma. This indication should expand the addressable market from approximately 18,000 patients to approximately 27,000 patients, but actual net sales potential will likely (at least) double due to much longer duration of therapy. This is just my rough estimate, as we have not yet seen the data beyond the topline ORR and a 79% reduction in disease progression versus the control arm.

Epkinly is being shared with AbbVie (50:50 profit split in the U.S. and Japan and 22-26% royalties to Genmab in other territories), but it will still become a significant profit center for Genmab. I think that $5 billion+ in annual peak sales is reasonable and likely in the existing targeted indications, and this does not include the upside potential from its potential expansion into I&I (immunology and inflammation).

However, I would not count on I&I for now, as Genmab needs to convince AbbVie to do it, and it will also take quite a bit of time before this side of Epkinly’s pipeline matures. There is also the safety angle of these bispecifics, where the risk-benefit is very good for cancer patients but may not be for patients with non-life-threatening autoimmune diseases.

Rina-S is closely behind Epkinly with a first potential commercial launch in 2027 and acasunlimab in 2028 (if it delivers positive data). You can see the estimated launch dates across product candidates and indications in the presentation slide below.

Considering the expected launch cadence and the sales potential of these products, coupled with increasing royalties from other partnered products, I expect a smooth and painless transition when Darzalex royalties disappear.

Now, consider this picture for 2031 (my estimates):

  • Epkinly net sales and royalties of $4 billion.
  • Rina-S net sales of $1.5 billion.
  • Tivdak net sales of $300-400 million.
  • Acasunlimab’s net sales? I do not know. It could be $1 billion+, but it could also be zero. I am not convinced by the existing data and want to see more. Genmab does set a high bar for progress of its candidates, so I do give them the benefit of the doubt here for moving acasunlimab into phase 3 development in second line+ NSCLC. The company also seems convinced enough by the NSCLC data to start a new trial phase 2 trial in melanoma.
  • Royalties on Kesimpta, Rybrevant, Tecvayli, Talvey, Tepezza, and the Mim8 candidate for hemophilia partnered with Novo Nordisk that should be approved next year are likely to be close to or above $1.5 billion (this does not include Epkinly royalties from AbbVie territories, which I have included in the Epkinly estimate above).
  • Revenues from in-licensed or acquired products – I will leave this open, but this could (should) be a meaningful source of revenue by 2031.
  • Significantly expanded pipeline through internal efforts (there are currently 7 ongoing early-stage programs) and business development, in oncology and I&I, with several candidates in either late-stage development or launch-ready in 2032/33.
  • Cash balance of $4-5 billion (from $3 billion today) as the company will be active on the business development front, and as I expect it to continue to opportunistically buy back shares. I estimate that Genmab will generate more than $10 billion in free cash flow between now and 2031 (meaning $8-9 billion is spent on M&A and/or buybacks).
  • Darzalex royalties go to zero.

So, $7 billion+ in revenue (and probably more versus the current consensus estimate of $5.37 billion) with no contribution of Darzalex royalties, likely $3 billion+ in net income considering the current and expected cost structure, plus a significantly expanded pipeline versus today (oncology and I&I versus only oncology today), strong growth outlook, and a robust balance sheet.

What is this kind of company worth in 2031? Very likely multiples of the current valuation, and with a present value that is well above the current share price. With my long-term growth estimates well above the Street consensus, I would value the stock close to the high end of the analyst price target range (the highest price target is $46) and above the highest price target based on somewhat more aggressive assumptions that include some contribution of either acasunlimab or current early-stage pipeline assets and in-licensed or acquired products.

And I get this by applying a low or no growth earnings multiple of 15 and would expect Genmab to be assigned a higher multiple by the market at the time. Doing this allows some cushion for revenue and profit underperformance.

What are the risks?

There are no Darzalex-related risks anymore. There is unlikely to be any kind of competition that threatens the royalty stream into the late 2020s and early 2030s. In fact, the use of Darzalex in earlier lines of multiple myeloma continues to expand.

Similarly, the market landscape in FL and DLBCL is not seeing significant innovation beyond Epkinly’s CD20xCD3 class of competitors – Roche and Regeneron represent the key in-class competition, but both are either behind or less convenient (or both). And it takes 8-10 years to break into earlier lines of FL and DLBCL, given the length of these trials, and a few more years for real commercial disruption. As such, I do not see real threats to Epkinly beyond Roche and Regeneron anytime soon.

Similarly, Kesimpta looks pretty safe in multiple sclerosis over the lifetime of its patents and royalties to Genmab, while the other royalty products are not large enough to meaningfully alter the outlook for Genmab (unless Rybrevant becomes a lot larger than anyone expects).

The picture is not as clear in markets for Rina-S and acasunlimab. I envision continued market evolution in these markets. Rina-S is also being challenged in court by AbbVie for trade secret misappropriation from former AbbVie employees that are now Genmab employees. I expect Genmab will prevail but cannot say that with absolute certainty.

The company could make poor business development decisions and in-license and/or acquire assets that fail in the clinic or that fail commercially.

And last but not least, all the biopharma macro risks apply to Genmab, though I do not see a lot of pricing risk for any of its products, as oncology products are expensive everywhere.

Conclusion

Shares of Genmab have advanced in the last few weeks, but I believe this is just the beginning and that it is a matter of time before the stock delivers market- and industry-beating gains.

The Darzalex-related disappointments are in the rearview mirror, and while there are still other risks for the long-term investment thesis and something else can go wrong, I like the risk-reward at current levels quite a bit and expect significant net-positive news flow over the next 5+ years, with steady expansion of the wholly-owned commercial portfolio and pipeline through internal efforts and business development.
